title: AML Agents Briefing — Wednesday, 10 June 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing for Tuesday the tenth of June 2026. This is your audio overview of the most material AML and financial crime developments across the Asia-Pacific region today. Full detail, the obligation register, news sources, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Big day, Alex. We have agentic AI moving into payments and mortgage underwriting, crypto correlation signals worth watching, and some important obligations on the horizon for compliance teams in Australia, Indonesia, and Hong Kong.

ALEX: Let's start with the story that deserves the most attention this morning. Two separate developments dropped today that together mark a real shift in how autonomous AI systems interact with financial flows. ING has deployed agentic AI to automate mortgage decisions in the Netherlands, and PayPal has partnered with UK fashion platform Hey Savi to launch an agentic commerce platform with in-app checkout. On the surface these look like product announcements. For compliance professionals, they are something more significant.

JORDAN: What's the AML angle on the mortgage side, Alex? ING's announcement didn't foreground it.

ALEX: That's exactly the issue. When an AI agent makes a credit decision autonomously, the know-your-customer and source-of-wealth checks feeding that decision have to be just as robust as they would be in a human-assisted process. If the automated pipeline is pulling from a customer profile that hasn't been refreshed, or if the agent is not triggering enhanced due diligence for higher-risk applicants, you have a compliance gap that supervisors will find during their next thematic review of automated onboarding and credit processes.

JORDAN: And the PayPal and Hey Savi partnership compounds that. An AI agent executing payments on behalf of a consumer, inside a third-party commerce environment, raises immediate questions about who owns the customer due diligence obligation, and whether the transaction monitoring on the payment leg can see enough of the chain to detect structuring or mule activity.

ALEX: Precisely. FATF Recommendation 10 on customer due diligence and Recommendation 16 on wire transfers don't disappear because a payment is initiated by an agent rather than a human. The underlying obligations attach to the financial institution in the chain.

JORDAN: So what should listeners do this week? If you are the MLRO at a bank or payments firm exploring agentic AI in any customer-facing or payment-execution workflow, pull your automated process inventory now. Map which AML controls are human-in-the-loop and which are fully automated. Before you take anything to your risk committee, confirm that your customer due diligence refresh triggers and your transaction monitoring rules are firing correctly for agent-initiated events, not just human-initiated ones. That's a gap most control frameworks have not yet addressed explicitly.

ALEX: Moving on, two crypto market developments today are worth a brief read-across for compliance teams. Bitcoin and gold fell simultaneously on renewed rate-hike expectations, and separately, data from CoinDesk shows Bitcoin exchange-traded fund assets under management in the United States have not grown since November 2024, despite the institutional inflow narrative that followed the US election.

JORDAN: The correlation point matters for typology work. When Bitcoin and gold move together on macro signals, the risk narratives around them also converge. Compliance officers who cover both traditional commodity desks and virtual asset service provider relationships need to be alert to the possibility that clients repositioning across both asset classes are doing so in ways that look normal on each leg individually but are harder to detect across the full picture.

ALEX: And the stagnant exchange-traded fund flows are a useful calibration point. If your virtual asset risk appetite is still premised on the assumption of rapid institutional growth in this space, the data says that assumption needs revisiting.

JORDAN: Agreed. Add that to your next virtual asset risk assessment refresh. It affects your customer risk scoring if you have institutional clients with significant Bitcoin exchange-traded fund exposure.

ALEX: Now for what to watch. Jordan, take us through the obligations horizon.

JORDAN: Three items for compliance teams across the region. First, for Australian firms registered with AUSTRAC, your annual compliance report for the 2024 calendar year is a filing you should have in hand. Late lodgement attracts daily civil penalties and a misleading attestation carries criminal liability under section 137 of the AML and CTF Act. If that report has not been lodged, escalate today. Second, Indonesian reporting entities under POJK 12 of 2017 should be completing their independent annual review of the AML and CFT programme covering 2024. Findings go to the Risk Oversight Committee and the Board of Commissioners. If your independent reviewer has not yet been commissioned, that is urgent. Third, Hong Kong-regulated institutions under the HKMA AML and CFT Guideline section 11 have the annual self-assessment return covering 2024. That goes via the HKMA-prescribed Excel template with Internal Audit validation. Check your submission timeline now.

ALEX: And on the horizon scanning side, two AUSTRAC items deserve attention. Detailed Tranche 2 guidance for legal practitioners has been published, covering source-of-wealth diligence in conveyancing and legal professional privilege carve-outs. If you are in a firm advising legal practices on their new obligations, that guidance is the reference document. And AUSTRAC's forty-seven million Australian dollar civil penalty against a major digital currency exchange for systemic transaction monitoring failures is a live enforcement signal. The read-across for any AUSTRAC-registered digital currency exchange is straightforward: mule account detection and suspicious matter report timeliness are active enforcement priorities right now.

JORDAN: For listeners, the Bank Negara Malaysia typology bulletin on investment scams and mule accounts routed through digital banks and crypto exchanges is also fresh. The indicators highlighted are rapid velocity spikes post-onboarding and inbound transfers from multiple unrelated retail senders. Review those in your transaction monitoring rule set this week.

JORDAN: Full detail on all of today's stories, the complete obligation register, and horizon scanning are at amlagents dot streamlit dot app. Worth bookmarking if it isn't already.

ALEX: That's the briefing for Tuesday the tenth of June. We'll be back tomorrow. Stay sharp.
